## Key Takeaways

-

## Content

Foundation models are reshaping the economics of the software industry in ways that few predicted even two years ago. The marginal cost of intelligence is falling rapidly, but the fixed costs of training continue to rise.

The key tension is between scale and specialization. Large general-purpose models benefit from economies of scale in training data and compute, but specialized fine-tuned models can outperform them on narrow tasks at a fraction of the inference cost.

Three economic dynamics are worth watching:

1. **Training cost concentration** — Only a handful of organizations can afford frontier model training runs, which now exceed $1B. This creates a natural oligopoly at the foundation layer.

2. **Inference cost deflation** — Competition and hardware improvements are driving inference costs down 10x per year. This is the single most important trend for AI adoption.

3. **Value capture shifting to applications** — As model capabilities commoditize, value increasingly accrues to companies that build domain-specific workflows, proprietary data flywheels, and user trust.

The winners in this new landscape will not necessarily be the best model builders, but rather the best system integrators — companies that combine foundation models with domain data, human expertise, and reliable infrastructure.
